questions.stem: Converting a future cash amount into its equivalent value today is called
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Present worth divides the future sum by $(1+i)^n$.

**Working.** This process of moving money backward in time is discounting.

**Answer.** discounting.

questions.topic: Time Value of Money — Effective Rate
questions.stem: When a nominal rate is compounded more than once per year, the effective annual rate is
options: always exactly double the nominal rate
questions.solution:
**Concept.** More frequent compounding earns interest on interest sooner.

**Working.** Hence $i_{eff}>r$ for $m>1$.

**Answer.** greater than the nominal rate.

questions.stem: The capital-recovery factor is the reciprocal of the
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $(A/P)$ converts a present sum to an annuity; $(P/A)$ does the reverse.

**Working.** Hence $(A/P)=1/(P/A)$.

**Answer.** uniform-series present-worth factor.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: The time value of money principle states that
options: a rupee today is worth more than a rupee in the future
money loses value only through theft
future rupees are always worth more than present rupees
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Present money can earn interest.

**Working.** Hence current rupees are worth more than the same future amount.

**Answer.** a rupee today is worth more than a rupee in the future.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: A perpetuity is
options: a loan repaid in two installments
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Its present worth is $A/i$.

**Working.** It is a uniform series with no end.

**Answer.** an annuity that continues indefinitely.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.topic: Time Value of Money — Continuous Compounding
questions.stem: For a nominal rate $r$ compounded continuously, the effective annual rate is
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Continuous limit of $(1+r/m)^m$ as $m\to\infty$ is $e^r$.

**Working.** Effective rate $=e^r-1$.

**Answer.** $e^{r}-1$.

questions.stem: In the early installments of an amortized loan, the payment consists mostly of
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Interest accrues on the large early outstanding balance.

**Working.** Early payments are mostly interest, later ones mostly principal.

**Answer.** interest.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: The factor $(F/P,\,i,\,n)=(1+i)^n$ is called the
questions.solution:
**Concept.** It converts a present single sum to its future value.

**Working.** $(F/P)=(1+i)^n$ is the single-payment compound-amount factor.

**Answer.** single-payment compound-amount factor.

options: its estimated resale or scrap value at the end of its useful life
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Depreciation reduces value down to salvage.

**Working.** It is the recoverable value at retirement.

**Answer.** its estimated resale or scrap value at the end of its useful life.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: Which depreciation method generally charges the largest depreciation in the first year?
options: units of production with uniform output
questions.solution:
**Concept.** DDB is an accelerated method.

**Working.** It applies a high rate to the full early book value.

**Answer.** double declining balance.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: Depreciation in cost accounting is best described as
options: the systematic allocation of an asset's cost over its useful life
an actual cash outflow each year
the resale value of the asset
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Depreciation is a non-cash expense.

**Working.** It spreads the capital cost across the years of use.

**Answer.** the systematic allocation of an asset's cost over its useful life.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: At the break-even point,
options: total revenue equals total cost and profit is zero
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Break-even is where contribution exactly covers fixed cost.

**Working.** Revenue $=$ cost, so profit $=0$.

**Answer.** total revenue equals total cost and profit is zero.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: Fixed costs are those that
options: do not change with the level of output in the short run
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Examples: rent, salaries, depreciation.

**Working.** They remain constant regardless of volume.

**Answer.** do not change with the level of output in the short run.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: At a variable cost of $\textsf{Rs}\,60$/unit, the total variable cost for $10{,}000$ units is $\textsf{Rs}$ _____ .

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: The margin of safety is
options: the excess of actual (or budgeted) sales over break-even sales
the fixed cost divided by price
questions.solution:
**Concept.** It measures how far sales can fall before a loss.

**Working.** Margin of safety $=$ actual sales $-$ break-even sales.

**Answer.** the excess of actual sales over break-even sales.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: Actual sales are $20{,}000$ units and break-even is $12{,}500$ units. The margin of safety is _____ units.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: A sunk cost is
options: a past cost that is irrelevant to current decisions
a future cost that varies with output
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Sunk costs are already incurred and unrecoverable.

**Working.** They should be excluded from forward-looking decisions.

**Answer.** a past cost that is irrelevant to current decisions.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering

questions.stem: At a depletion rate of $\textsf{Rs}\,20$/tonne, the depletion charge for a year mining $50{,}000$ tonnes is $\textsf{Rs}$ _____ .

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: Depletion (as distinct from depreciation) applies to
options: natural resources / wasting assets such as ore bodies
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Depletion allocates the cost of a consumed resource.

**Working.** It applies to minerals, oil and other wasting assets.

**Answer.** natural resources / wasting assets such as ore bodies.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: Annual depreciation is $\textsf{Rs}\,45{,}000$ and the corporate tax rate is $30\%$. The annual depreciation tax shield is $\textsf{Rs}$ _____ .

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: Accelerated depreciation is often preferred for tax purposes because it
options: provides larger deductions early, deferring tax to later years
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Early deductions defer tax payments.

**Working.** Deferral has positive time value (cash kept longer).

**Answer.** provides larger deductions early, deferring tax to later years.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering

questions.stem: A project requires $\textsf{Rs}\,1{,}00{,}000$ now and returns $\textsf{Rs}\,30{,}000$ at the end of each year for $5$ years. At $10\%$, its net present value is $\textsf{Rs}$ _____ . Round to the nearest rupee.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: Using the net-present-value criterion, an independent project is acceptable when its NPV is
options: greater than or equal to zero
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Positive NPV means returns exceed the required rate.

**Working.** Accept if $\text{NPV}\ge 0$.

**Answer.** greater than or equal to zero.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: A project costs $\textsf{Rs}\,1{,}00{,}000$ and returns a uniform $\textsf{Rs}\,25{,}000$ per year. The simple payback period is _____ years.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: A project costs $\textsf{Rs}\,1{,}00{,}000$ and returns $\textsf{Rs}\,30{,}000$ per year. The simple payback period is _____ years. Round to two decimals.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: A major limitation of the simple payback period is that it
options: ignores the time value of money and cash flows after payback
requires an interest rate to compute
cannot be applied to uniform cash flows
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Payback only counts time to recover the outlay.

**Working.** It disregards discounting and later returns.

**Answer.** ignores the time value of money and cash flows after payback.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: A project has present-worth benefits of $\textsf{Rs}\,1{,}50{,}000$ and present-worth costs of $\textsf{Rs}\,1{,}00{,}000$. The benefit-cost ratio is _____ .

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: A project is considered economically justified when its benefit-cost ratio is
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $B/C\ge 1$ means benefits at least cover costs.

**Working.** Accept if $B/C\ge1$.

**Answer.** greater than or equal to 1.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: A project's present worth of future cash flows is $\textsf{Rs}\,1{,}13{,}724$ for an initial investment of $\textsf{Rs}\,1{,}00{,}000$. The profitability index is _____ . Round to two decimals.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: In engineering economy, MARR stands for
questions.solution:
**Concept.** MARR is the hurdle rate for accepting projects.

**Working.** It is the Minimum Attractive Rate of Return.

**Answer.** Minimum Attractive Rate of Return.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: A project costs $\textsf{Rs}\,50{,}000$ and returns $\textsf{Rs}\,20{,}000$ per year for $4$ years. At a MARR of $12\%$ (so $(P/A,12\%,4)=3.0373$), its NPV is $\textsf{Rs}$ _____ . Round to the nearest rupee.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: A project costs $\textsf{Rs}\,1{,}00{,}000$ and returns $\textsf{Rs}\,40{,}000$ per year at $10\%$. The discounted payback period is _____ years. Round to two decimals.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Accumulate discounted cash flows until they equal the investment.

**Working.** Cumulative PW: yr1 $36364$, yr2 $69421$, yr3 $99474$, yr4 adds $27321$.
$$t=3+\frac{100000-99474}{27321}=3.02.$$

**Answer.** $\approx 3.02$ years.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: Among mutually exclusive projects evaluated at the same MARR, the preferred one is that with the
options: lowest initial cost regardless of returns
questions.solution:
**Concept.** NPV measures wealth added.

**Working.** Choose the alternative maximising NPV.

**Answer.** highest net present value.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: A project's NPV is $\textsf{Rs}\,13{,}724$ over a $5$-year life at $10\%$. Its equivalent uniform annual worth is $\textsf{Rs}$ _____ . Round to the nearest rupee.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: A project NPV of $\textsf{Rs}\,13{,}724$ at $10\%$ has, after $5$ years, an equivalent future worth of $\textsf{Rs}$ _____ . Round to the nearest rupee.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: The internal rate of return of a project is the discount rate at which
options: the net present value equals zero
the payback period is one year
questions.solution:
**Concept.** IRR is the breakeven discount rate.

**Working.** At IRR, $\text{NPV}=0$.

**Answer.** the net present value equals zero.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: A $\textsf{Rs}\,1{,}00{,}000$ investment yields a constant annual profit of $\textsf{Rs}\,25{,}000$. The simple rate of return is _____ percent.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: A project costs $\textsf{Rs}\,1{,}00{,}000$, returns $\textsf{Rs}\,30{,}000$/year for $5$ years and has a salvage of $\textsf{Rs}\,20{,}000$ at year $5$. At $10\%$, its NPV is $\textsf{Rs}$ _____ . Round to the nearest rupee.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Add the discounted salvage to the annuity NPV.

**Working.**
$$-100000+30000(3.7908)+\frac{20000}{1.61051}=-100000+113724+12418=26142.$$

**Answer.** $\textsf{Rs}\,26{,}142$.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: For a project with positive future cash flows, increasing the discount rate will
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Higher discount rates shrink the present worth of future flows.

**Working.** Hence NPV falls.

**Answer.** lower its net present value.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: Machine A ($\textsf{Rs}\,3{,}00{,}000$) and machine B ($\textsf{Rs}\,4{,}00{,}000$) both last $5$ years with no salvage at $10\%$ ($(A/P)=0.26380$). The annual cost advantage of A over B is $\textsf{Rs}$ _____ . Round to the nearest rupee.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: To compare alternatives with different service lives, the most convenient method is
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Annualising removes the effect of differing horizons.

**Working.** EUAC (or a common multiple of lives) gives a fair comparison.

**Answer.** the equivalent uniform annual cost.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: A project costs $\textsf{Rs}\,20{,}000$ and returns $\textsf{Rs}\,15{,}000$ at the end of years $1$ and $2$. At $10\%$, its NPV is $\textsf{Rs}$ _____ . Round to the nearest rupee.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Discount each receipt and subtract the cost.

**Working.**
$$-20000+\frac{15000}{1.10}+\frac{15000}{1.21}=-20000+13636+12397=6033.$$

**Answer.** $\textsf{Rs}\,6{,}033$.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: A project costs $\textsf{Rs}\,20{,}000$ and returns $\textsf{Rs}\,15{,}000$ at the end of years $1$ and $2$. The simple payback period is _____ years. Round to two decimals.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: The profitability index of a project is defined as
options: present worth of future cash flows divided by the initial investment
annual profit divided by salvage value
questions.solution:
**Concept.** PI relates value created to capital spent.

**Working.** $\text{PI}=\dfrac{\text{PW of future flows}}{\text{initial investment}}$.

**Answer.** present worth of future cash flows divided by the initial investment.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: A mine earns annual revenue of $\textsf{Rs}\,5{,}00{,}000$ with operating costs of $\textsf{Rs}\,3{,}20{,}000$. The net annual cash flow is $\textsf{Rs}$ _____ .
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Net cash flow $=$ revenue $-$ operating cost.

**Working.**
$$500000-320000=180000.$$

**Answer.** $\textsf{Rs}\,1{,}80{,}000$.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: A mine costs $\textsf{Rs}\,8{,}00{,}000$ and yields a net $\textsf{Rs}\,1{,}80{,}000$/year for $8$ years. At $12\%$ ($(P/A,12\%,8)=4.9676$), its NPV is $\textsf{Rs}$ _____ . Round to the nearest rupee.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: A negative net present value indicates that the project
options: earns less than the required rate of return and should be rejected
questions.solution:
**Concept.** NPV $<0$ means discounted benefits fall short of cost.

**Working.** The project fails the MARR test.

**Answer.** earns less than the required rate of return and should be rejected.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: A project earns an average annual profit of $\textsf{Rs}\,18{,}000$ on an average investment of $\textsf{Rs}\,50{,}000$. The accounting rate of return is _____ percent.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: A project costs $\textsf{Rs}\,50{,}000$ and returns $\textsf{Rs}\,20{,}000$/year for $4$ years. Given $(P/A,20\%,4)=2.5887$ and $(P/A,22\%,4)=2.4936$, the IRR by interpolation is _____ percent. Round to one decimal.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: Sensitivity analysis in project evaluation examines
options: how the outcome (e.g. NPV) changes as input variables are varied
the legal ownership of the asset
questions.solution:
**Concept.** It tests robustness to uncertain inputs.

**Working.** Each variable (price, cost, life) is varied to see the NPV response.

**Answer.** how the outcome changes as input variables are varied.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: A project has a fixed capital cost of $\textsf{Rs}\,7{,}00{,}000$ and requires working capital of $\textsf{Rs}\,1{,}00{,}000$. The total initial investment is $\textsf{Rs}$ _____ .
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Total investment $=$ fixed capital $+$ working capital.

**Working.**
$$700000+100000=800000.$$

**Answer.** $\textsf{Rs}\,8{,}00{,}000$.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: Opportunity cost is
options: the value of the best alternative forgone
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Choosing one option means giving up the next-best.

**Working.** Its forgone value is the opportunity cost.

**Answer.** the value of the best alternative forgone.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: A project has present-worth benefits of $\textsf{Rs}\,2{,}50{,}000$, an initial cost of $\textsf{Rs}\,1{,}50{,}000$ and present-worth O&M of $\textsf{Rs}\,50{,}000$. The benefit-cost ratio is _____ . Round to two decimals.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: A mine has revenue minus operating cost of $\textsf{Rs}\,1{,}80{,}000$, depreciation of $\textsf{Rs}\,45{,}000$ and a tax rate of $30\%$. The after-tax cash flow is $\textsf{Rs}$ _____ .

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: A surface mine removes $30{,}00{,}000$ tonnes of waste to extract $10{,}00{,}000$ tonnes of ore. The stripping ratio is _____ .
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $\text{SR}=\dfrac{\text{waste}}{\text{ore}}$.

**Working.**
$$\frac{3000000}{1000000}=3.$$

**Answer.** $3$ (tonnes of waste per tonne of ore).

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: Ore is worth $\textsf{Rs}\,50$/t after deducting $\textsf{Rs}\,20$/t mining-and-processing cost; waste removal costs $\textsf{Rs}\,10$/t. The break-even stripping ratio is _____ .

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: As an open-pit mine is deepened, the stripping ratio generally
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Deeper benches expose more waste per tonne of ore.

**Working.** The waste-to-ore ratio rises with depth.

**Answer.** increases.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: A pit will produce $4$ million tonnes of ore and $12$ million tonnes of waste. The overall stripping ratio is _____ .

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: With a stripping ratio of $2.5$ and an ore reserve of $20{,}00{,}000$ tonnes, the waste to be removed is _____ tonnes.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: The cut-off grade of a deposit is
options: the grade that just separates ore from waste (break-even grade)
the highest grade in the deposit
the average grade of the ore
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Below the cut-off, material is uneconomic.

**Working.** It is the break-even grade dividing ore from waste.

**Answer.** the grade that just separates ore from waste.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: The cost to mine and process is $\textsf{Rs}\,36$/t, metal price is $\textsf{Rs}\,8000$/t and recovery is $90\%$. The break-even cut-off grade is _____ percent. Round to two decimals.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: $1{,}00{,}000$ tonnes of ore assays $1.5\%$ metal. The contained metal is _____ tonnes.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: Of $1500$ tonnes of contained metal, $90\%$ is recovered in processing. The recoverable metal is _____ tonnes.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: An ore block has a volume of $5{,}00{,}000\,\text{m}^3$ and a bulk density of $2.7\,\text{t/m}^3$. Its tonnage is _____ tonnes.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: The tonnage factor relates
options: volume of rock to its tonnage (via bulk density)
questions.solution:
**Concept.** It is the bulk density (or its reciprocal).

**Working.** Tonnage $=$ volume $\div$ tonnage factor (or $\times$ density).

**Answer.** volume of rock to its tonnage.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: Block A is $1000$ t at $2\%$ and block B is $3000$ t at $1\%$. The tonnage-weighted average grade is _____ percent.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: $1000$ tonnes of ore at $3\%$ is mined with $100$ tonnes of zero-grade waste dilution. The diluted grade is _____ percent. Round to two decimals.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: If $100$ tonnes of waste dilutes $1000$ tonnes of ore, the percentage dilution is _____ percent. Round to two decimals.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: In order of increasing geological confidence, mineral resources are classified as
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Confidence rises from inferred to measured.

**Working.** The correct increasing order is inferred $\to$ indicated $\to$ measured.

**Answer.** inferred, indicated, measured.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: A tabular deposit has plan area $50{,}000\,\text{m}^2$, thickness $5\,\text{m}$ and density $2.5\,\text{t/m}^3$. The in-situ reserve is _____ tonnes.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: A reserve of $1$ crore ($10{,}000{,}000$) tonnes is mined at $10{,}00{,}000$ tonnes per year. The mine life is _____ years.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: Taylor's rule in mine planning provides an estimate of the
options: optimum mine life (and production rate) from the reserve tonnage
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Taylor's empirical law links life to tonnage.

**Working.** It gives an approximate optimum mine life from reserves.

**Answer.** optimum mine life from the reserve tonnage.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: Using Taylor's rule, life $\approx 6.5\,(\text{tonnage in Mt})^{0.25}$ years. For a reserve of $16$ million tonnes, the estimated mine life is _____ years.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: An annual output of $30{,}00{,}000$ tonnes is mined over $300$ working days. The daily production is _____ tonnes.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: Removing $50{,}00{,}000$ tonnes of waste at $\textsf{Rs}\,50$/t costs $\textsf{Rs}$ _____ .

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: The ultimate (economic) pit limit is reached where
options: the incremental stripping cost equals the incremental ore value
all the ore is exhausted regardless of cost
the bench height equals the burden
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Beyond this depth, mining the next ore costs more than it returns.

**Working.** This is the break-even stripping-ratio boundary.

**Answer.** the incremental stripping cost equals the incremental ore value.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: Mining $20{,}00{,}000$ tonnes of ore at $\textsf{Rs}\,100$/t costs $\textsf{Rs}$ _____ .

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: Ore at $2\%$ ($=20\,\text{kg/t}$) is processed at $90\%$ recovery with a metal price of $\textsf{Rs}\,400$/kg. The net smelter return per tonne of ore is $\textsf{Rs}$ _____ .
questions.solution:
**Concept.** NSR $=$ grade $\times$ recovery $\times$ price.

**Working.**
$$20\times0.90\times400=18\times400=7200.$$

**Answer.** $\textsf{Rs}\,7200$ per tonne.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: In open-pit terminology, the bench height is
options: the vertical height of a single working level
the horizontal width of a haul road
the depth of a blast hole
the angle of the pit wall
questions.solution:
**Concept.** A bench is one mining level.

**Working.** Its vertical dimension is the bench height.

**Answer.** the vertical height of a single working level.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: A pit $120\,\text{m}$ deep is worked in $12\,\text{m}$ benches. The number of benches is _____ .

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: Approximating a pit as a prism of plan area $1{,}00{,}000\,\text{m}^2$ and depth $120\,\text{m}$, its volume is _____ million m$^3$.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: A block model used in computerised pit optimisation is
options: a 3D grid of blocks, each assigned grade and economic value
a 2D contour map of the surface
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Optimisers (e.g. Lerchs-Grossmann) operate on blocks.

**Working.** Each block carries tonnage, grade and net value.

**Answer.** a 3D grid of blocks, each assigned grade and economic value.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: A $1000$-tonne block has a net smelter return of $\textsf{Rs}\,7200$/t and a mining-plus-processing cost of $\textsf{Rs}\,5000$/t. Its net block value is $\textsf{Rs}$ _____ .

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: Ore yields an NSR of $\textsf{Rs}\,7200$/t against an ore cost of $\textsf{Rs}\,5000$/t; waste removal costs $\textsf{Rs}\,1100$/t. The break-even stripping ratio is _____ . Round to two decimals.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: The principal objective of long-term production scheduling is to
options: maximise net present value subject to capacity, grade and blending constraints
minimise the number of benches only
keep the cut-off grade at zero
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Scheduling sequences extraction over time.

**Working.** It targets maximum NPV within operating constraints.

**Answer.** maximise net present value subject to constraints.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: To blend $2\%$ and $1\%$ ores into a $1.4\%$ feed, the fraction of the $2\%$ ore required is _____ percent.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: The lowest bench exposes $6{,}00{,}000$ tonnes of waste to recover $2{,}00{,}000$ tonnes of ore. Its incremental stripping ratio is _____ .

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: Raising the cut-off grade of a deposit generally
options: increases the average grade but reduces the ore tonnage
has no effect on grade or tonnage
questions.solution:
**Concept.** A higher cut-off rejects more low-grade material.

**Working.** Mean grade rises while tonnage above cut-off falls.

**Answer.** increases the average grade but reduces the ore tonnage.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: An in-situ reserve of $1$ crore ($10{,}000{,}000$) tonnes is mined at $85\%$ recovery. The recoverable reserve is _____ tonnes.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: In CPM network analysis, the critical path is
options: the longest path through the network, having zero total float
the shortest path through the network
the path with the most activities
questions.solution:
**Concept.** The critical path governs project duration.

**Working.** It is the longest path; its activities have zero total float.

**Answer.** the longest path with zero total float.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: For an activity with optimistic $t_o=4$, most-likely $t_m=6$ and pessimistic $t_p=8$ (days), the PERT expected time is _____ days.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: For $t_o=4$ and $t_p=8$ days, the PERT variance of the activity is _____ . Round to two decimals.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: The critical path consists of three activities of duration $5$, $8$ and $7$ days. The project duration is _____ days.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Project duration $=$ sum of critical activity times.

**Working.**
$$5+8+7=20.$$

**Answer.** $20$ days.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: The total float of an activity is computed as
options: Latest Start minus Earliest Start (LS - ES)
the sum of all successor durations
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Total float $=$ LS $-$ ES $=$ LF $-$ EF.

**Working.** It is the slack available without delaying the project.

**Answer.** Latest Start minus Earliest Start.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: An activity has Earliest Start $=4$ and Latest Start $=7$ days. Its total float is _____ days.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: The critical-path activities have variances $0.44$, $1.00$ and $0.56$. The project standard deviation is _____ days. Round to two decimals.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: An activity finishes earliest at $\text{EF}=10$; its successor starts earliest at $\text{ES}=12$. The free float of the activity is _____ days.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: Compared with CPM, the PERT technique is
options: probabilistic, using three time estimates per activity
concerned only with cost, not time
questions.solution:
**Concept.** PERT handles uncertainty.

**Working.** It uses optimistic/most-likely/pessimistic estimates; CPM uses a single deterministic time.

**Answer.** probabilistic, using three time estimates.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: A project has expected duration $20$ days and standard deviation $2$ days. The probability of finishing within $22$ days corresponds to $z=1.0$; using $\Phi(1.0)=0.8413$, this probability is _____ . Round to four decimals.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: In the M/M/1 queueing model, arrivals and service are assumed to follow
options: a Poisson arrival process and exponential service times
questions.solution:
**Concept.** M/M/1 denotes Markovian arrivals and service.

**Working.** Arrivals are Poisson; service times are exponential; one server.

**Answer.** Poisson arrivals, exponential service.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: Trucks arrive at a shovel at $\lambda=6$/hr and are loaded at $\mu=10$/hr. The utilisation $\rho$ is _____ .

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: For an M/M/1 queue with $\rho=0.6$, the average number of units in the system $L_s$ is _____ .

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: For the same M/M/1 queue ($\rho=0.6$), the average number waiting in the queue $L_q$ is _____ . Round to two decimals.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: With $\lambda=6$/hr and $\mu=10$/hr, the average waiting time in the queue $W_q$ is _____ hours. Round to two decimals.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: With $\lambda=6$/hr and $\mu=10$/hr, the average time spent in the system $W_s$ is _____ hours.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: In an M/M/1 queue, as the utilisation $\rho$ approaches $1$, the average queue length
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $L_q=\dfrac{\rho^2}{1-\rho}\to\infty$ as $\rho\to1$.

**Working.** The denominator vanishes, so the queue grows without bound.

**Answer.** tends to infinity.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: For an M/M/1 queue with $\rho=0.6$, the probability that the system is empty $P_0$ is _____ .

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: A linear-programming model requires that
options: the objective function and all constraints be linear
at least one constraint be quadratic
questions.solution:
**Concept.** LP is defined by linearity.

**Working.** Both objective and constraints must be linear in the decision variables.

**Answer.** the objective and all constraints be linear.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: Maximise $Z=3x+5y$ subject to $x\le4$, $y\le6$, $3x+2y\le18$, $x,y\ge0$. The optimum value of $Z$ is _____ .
questions.solution:
**Concept.** The optimum lies at a corner of the feasible region.

**Working.** Corners: $(0,6)\to30$, $(2,6)\to3(2)+5(6)=36$, $(4,3)\to27$, $(4,0)\to12$. Maximum at $(2,6)$.
$$Z_{\max}=36.$$

**Answer.** $36$.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: An unbounded feasible region in a linear program
options: may still yield a finite optimum depending on the objective direction
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Boundedness of the optimum depends on the objective.

**Working.** If the objective improves only towards a bounded face, a finite optimum exists.

**Answer.** may still yield a finite optimum.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: Annual demand $D=10{,}000$ units, ordering cost $S=\textsf{Rs}\,50$/order and holding cost $H=\textsf{Rs}\,4$/unit-year. The economic order quantity is _____ units.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: With $D=10{,}000$ units/year and $\text{EOQ}=500$ units, the number of orders placed per year is _____ .

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: At the EOQ ($20$ orders/year, $S=\textsf{Rs}\,50$), the annual ordering cost is $\textsf{Rs}$ _____ .

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: At the EOQ ($Q=500$, $H=\textsf{Rs}\,4$/unit-year), the annual holding cost is $\textsf{Rs}$ _____ .

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: At the economic order quantity, the annual
options: ordering cost equals the holding cost
questions.solution:
**Concept.** EOQ balances the two variable costs.

**Working.** $\dfrac{D}{Q}S=\dfrac{Q}{2}H$ at the optimum.

**Answer.** ordering cost equals holding cost.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: At the EOQ, ordering and holding costs are each $\textsf{Rs}\,1000$/year. The total variable inventory cost is $\textsf{Rs}$ _____ .

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: The reliability of two independent components in series equals
options: the product of their individual reliabilities
the larger of the two reliabilities
one minus the product of their reliabilities
questions.solution:
**Concept.** A series system works only if all components work.

**Working.** $R_s=R_1R_2$.

**Answer.** the product of their reliabilities.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: Two components of reliability $0.9$ and $0.8$ operate in series. The system reliability is _____ .

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: Two components of reliability $0.9$ and $0.8$ operate in parallel (redundant). The system reliability is _____ .

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: Two parallel units (each $0.9$) are in series with a single unit of reliability $0.8$. The overall reliability is _____ . Round to three decimals.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Combine the parallel block, then the series unit.

**Working.**
$$R_{\parallel}=1-(0.1)(0.1)=0.99,\qquad R=0.99\times0.8=0.792.$$

**Answer.** $0.792$.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: Equipment has a constant failure rate $\lambda=0.002$ per hour. Its mean time between failures (MTBF) is _____ hours.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: Under the Expected Monetary Value (EMV) criterion, the decision-maker selects the alternative with the
questions.solution:
**Concept.** EMV weights payoffs by their probabilities.

**Working.** The alternative with the highest expected value is chosen.

**Answer.** maximum expected monetary value.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: Alternative A yields $\textsf{Rs}\,100$ with probability $0.6$ and $-\textsf{Rs}\,20$ with probability $0.4$. Its expected monetary value is $\textsf{Rs}$ _____ .

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: Replacement theory is used to determine
options: the optimum time to replace equipment that deteriorates with age
the cut-off grade of an ore body
the critical path of a project
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Replacement balances rising maintenance cost against capital recovery.

**Working.** The optimum age minimises average annual cost.

**Answer.** the optimum time to replace deteriorating equipment.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: For the cost–revenue chart shown, the annual fixed cost is $\mathrm{Rs}\,1{,}00{,}000$, the selling price is $\mathrm{Rs}\,50$ per unit and the variable cost is $\mathrm{Rs}\,30$ per unit. The break-even output, in units, is _____.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: The cash-flow diagram shows an initial investment of $\mathrm{Rs}\,5{,}00{,}000$ followed by uniform annual net cash inflows of $\mathrm{Rs}\,1{,}25{,}000$. The simple payback period, in years, is _____.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 260 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='20' y1='80' x2='240' y2='80' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='40' y1='80' x2='40' y2='128' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#cdn)'/><g stroke='#16a34a' stroke-width='2'><line x1='90' y1='80' x2='90' y2='42' marker-end='url(#cup)'/><line x1='140' y1='80' x2='140' y2='42' marker-end='url(#cup)'/><line x1='190' y1='80' x2='190' y2='42' marker-end='url(#cup)'/><line x1='230' y1='80' x2='230' y2='42' marker-end='url(#cup)'/></g><defs><marker id='cdn' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='4' refY='6' orient='auto'><path d='M0 0 L4 6 L8 0 Z' fill='#dc2626'/></marker><marker id='cup' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='4' refY='0' orient='auto'><path d='M0 6 L4 0 L8 6 Z' fill='#16a34a'/></marker></defs><text x='18' y='100' font-size='8' fill='#dc2626'>500k out</text><text x='95' y='36' font-size='8' fill='#16a34a'>125k / yr in</text></svg>
figure.caption: Cash-flow diagram

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Simple payback $=\dfrac{\text{investment}}{\text{annual net cash inflow}}$.

**Working.**
$$T=\frac{5{,}00{,}000}{1{,}25{,}000}=4\ \text{years}$$

**Answer.** $4$ years.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: On the time line shown, a sum of $\mathrm{Rs}\,121$ is received at the end of year 2. At a discount rate of $10\%$ per annum, its present value (at year 0), in Rs, is _____.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 120' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='20' y1='60' x2='220' y2='60' stroke='#475569'/><g fill='#1f2937'><circle cx='40' cy='60' r='3'/><circle cx='130' cy='60' r='3'/><circle cx='210' cy='60' r='3'/></g><text x='36' y='50' font-size='9'>0</text><text x='126' y='50' font-size='9'>1</text><text x='206' y='50' font-size='9'>2</text><text x='176' y='86' font-size='9' fill='#16a34a'>FV = 121</text><text x='100' y='102' font-size='9'>i = 10%</text></svg>
figure.caption: Discounting a future cash flow

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: The cumulative-production graph shows a mineable reserve of 10 Mt extracted at a uniform rate of 2 Mt per year. The life of the mine, in years, is _____.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 170' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='30' y1='140' x2='220' y2='140' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='30' y1='140' x2='30' y2='20' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='30' y1='140' x2='190' y2='30' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='30' y1='30' x2='220' y2='30' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-dasharray='4 3'/><text x='140' y='26' font-size='9' fill='#dc2626'>reserve 10 Mt</text><text x='100' y='158' font-size='9'>years</text><text x='38' y='95' font-size='9' fill='#2563eb'>2 Mt/yr</text></svg>
figure.caption: Cumulative production vs time

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Mine life $=\dfrac{\text{mineable reserve}}{\text{annual production rate}}$.

**Working.**
$$L=\frac{10}{2}=5\ \text{years}$$

**Answer.** $5$ years.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: In the cost–revenue chart shown, the operation makes a profit when the output is

figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='30' y1='150' x2='220' y2='150' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='30' y1='150' x2='30' y2='20' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='30' y1='110' x2='220' y2='50' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='2'/><text x='150' y='50' font-size='9' fill='#dc2626'>total cost</text><line x1='30' y1='150' x2='220' y2='30' stroke='#16a34a' stroke-width='2'/><text x='150' y='32' font-size='9' fill='#16a34a'>revenue</text><circle cx='150' cy='70' r='3' fill='#1f2937'/><text x='150' y='84' font-size='8'>BEP</text><text x='110' y='170' font-size='9'>output</text></svg>
figure.caption: Cost and revenue lines

options: greater than the break-even output, where revenue exceeds total cost
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Profit occurs where the revenue line lies above the total-cost line.

**Reasoning.** To the right of the break-even point the revenue line is above the cost line, so total revenue exceeds total cost and the operation is profitable; to the left it makes a loss.

**Answer.** Greater than the break-even output.

questions.subject: Mine Economics & Planning
questions.stem: In the activity-on-node network below (durations in weeks), determine the project duration (length of the critical path) in weeks.

figure.caption: Project network
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 300 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><circle cx='30' cy='80' r='14' fill='#e0f2fe' stroke='#475569'/><text x='30' y='84' font-size='9' text-anchor='middle'>S</text><circle cx='130' cy='40' r='16' fill='#fef3c7' stroke='#b45309'/><text x='130' y='44' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>A=2</text><circle cx='130' cy='120' r='16' fill='#fef3c7' stroke='#b45309'/><text x='130' y='124' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>B=4</text><circle cx='230' cy='40' r='16' fill='#fef3c7' stroke='#b45309'/><text x='230' y='44' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>C=3</text><circle cx='230' cy='120' r='16' fill='#fef3c7' stroke='#b45309'/><text x='230' y='124' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>D=5</text><circle cx='285' cy='80' r='12' fill='#e0f2fe' stroke='#475569'/><text x='285' y='84' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>E</text><g stroke='#475569' fill='none'><path d='M44 74 L114 46'/><path d='M44 86 L114 114'/><path d='M146 40 L214 40'/><path d='M146 120 L214 120'/><path d='M246 46 L275 74'/><path d='M246 114 L275 86'/></g></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** The project duration equals the length of the longest (critical) path through the network.

**Working.**
$$S\text{–}A\text{–}C\text{–}E = 2+3 = 5,\quad S\text{–}B\text{–}D\text{–}E = 4+5 = 9$$

**Answer.** Critical path S–B–D–E gives a project duration of **9 weeks**.

questions.subject: Mine Economics & Planning
questions.stem: For the same network, determine the total float (in weeks) available on the non-critical chain A–C.

figure.caption: Project network
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 300 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><circle cx='30' cy='80' r='14' fill='#e0f2fe' stroke='#475569'/><text x='30' y='84' font-size='9' text-anchor='middle'>S</text><circle cx='130' cy='40' r='16' fill='#fef3c7' stroke='#b45309'/><text x='130' y='44' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>A=2</text><circle cx='130' cy='120' r='16' fill='#fef3c7' stroke='#b45309'/><text x='130' y='124' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>B=4</text><circle cx='230' cy='40' r='16' fill='#fef3c7' stroke='#b45309'/><text x='230' y='44' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>C=3</text><circle cx='230' cy='120' r='16' fill='#fef3c7' stroke='#b45309'/><text x='230' y='124' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>D=5</text><circle cx='285' cy='80' r='12' fill='#e0f2fe' stroke='#475569'/><text x='285' y='84' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>E</text><g stroke='#475569' fill='none'><path d='M44 74 L114 46'/><path d='M44 86 L114 114'/><path d='M146 40 L214 40'/><path d='M146 120 L214 120'/><path d='M246 46 L275 74'/><path d='M246 114 L275 86'/></g></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Total float on a chain equals the critical-path length minus the length of that chain.

**Working.**
$$F = 9 - (2+3) = 9 - 5 = 4$$

**Answer.** The A–C chain has **4 weeks** of total float.

questions.subject: Mine Economics & Planning
questions.stem: For the activity shown with optimistic, most-likely and pessimistic time estimates, compute the PERT expected time $t_e$ (weeks).

figure.caption: PERT activity
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 260 110' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><circle cx='40' cy='55' r='14' fill='#e0f2fe' stroke='#475569'/><circle cx='220' cy='55' r='14' fill='#e0f2fe' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='54' y1='55' x2='204' y2='55' stroke='#475569' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#pe)'/><defs><marker id='pe' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0 0 L6 3 L0 6 Z' fill='#475569'/></marker></defs><text x='130' y='42' font-size='9' text-anchor='middle'>to=2, tm=5, tp=8</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** PERT expected time uses a weighted beta estimate.

**Working.**
$$t_e = \frac{t_o + 4t_m + t_p}{6} = \frac{2 + 4(5) + 8}{6} = \frac{30}{6} = 5$$

**Answer.** $t_e = \mathbf{5}$ weeks.

questions.subject: Mine Economics & Planning
questions.stem: For the same activity ($t_o=2$, $t_m=5$, $t_p=8$ weeks), compute the variance of the activity duration (weeks²).

figure.caption: PERT activity
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 260 110' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><circle cx='40' cy='55' r='14' fill='#e0f2fe' stroke='#475569'/><circle cx='220' cy='55' r='14' fill='#e0f2fe' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='54' y1='55' x2='204' y2='55' stroke='#475569' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#pe)'/><defs><marker id='pe' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0 0 L6 3 L0 6 Z' fill='#475569'/></marker></defs><text x='130' y='42' font-size='9' text-anchor='middle'>to=2, tm=5, tp=8</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** PERT activity variance is $\sigma^2 = \left(\dfrac{t_p - t_o}{6}\right)^2$.

**Working.**
$$\sigma^2 = \left(\frac{8-2}{6}\right)^2 = (1)^2 = 1$$

**Answer.** Variance $= \mathbf{1}$ week².

questions.subject: Mine Economics & Planning
questions.stem: Using the inventory saw-tooth model with annual demand $D=1000$ units, ordering cost $S=50$ per order and holding cost $H=10$ per unit-year, compute the Economic Order Quantity (units).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** The EOQ minimises the sum of ordering and holding costs.

**Working.**
$$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}} = \sqrt{\frac{2(1000)(50)}{10}} = \sqrt{10000} = 100$$

**Answer.** EOQ $= \mathbf{100}$ units.

questions.subject: Mine Economics & Planning
questions.stem: For the linear-programming feasible region shown (a bounded convex polygon), where does the optimal value of a linear objective function occur?

figure.caption: LP feasible region
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 200 180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='30' y1='150' x2='190' y2='150' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='30' y1='150' x2='30' y2='20' stroke='#475569'/><polygon points='30,150 30,70 90,40 150,90 150,150' fill='#bfdbfe' stroke='#1d4ed8'/><g fill='#1f2937'><circle cx='90' cy='40' r='3'/><circle cx='150' cy='90' r='3'/><circle cx='30' cy='70' r='3'/></g><text x='66' y='112' font-size='9'>feasible region</text></svg>

options: At a corner (vertex) of the feasible region
At the centroid of the region
questions.solution:
**Concept.** For a linear objective over a convex polygon, the optimum is attained at an extreme point.

**Reasoning.** By the fundamental theorem of linear programming, an optimal solution (if it exists for a bounded feasible region) always lies at a vertex of the feasible polygon.

**Answer.** At a corner (vertex) of the feasible region.

questions.subject: Mine Economics & Planning
questions.stem: An asset costs ₹100000 with a salvage value of ₹10000 after a 9-year life. Using the straight-line method shown, compute the annual depreciation charge (₹).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Straight-line depreciation spreads the depreciable amount evenly over the life.

**Working.**
$$D = \frac{C - S}{n} = \frac{100000 - 10000}{9} = \frac{90000}{9} = 10000$$

**Answer.** ₹**10000** per year.

questions.subject: Mine Economics & Planning
questions.stem: Two components with reliabilities 0.9 and 0.8 are connected in series as shown. Compute the system reliability.

figure.caption: Series reliability block diagram
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 100' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='10' y1='50' x2='40' y2='50' stroke='#475569' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='40' y='35' width='60' height='30' fill='#e0f2fe' stroke='#475569'/><text x='70' y='54' font-size='9' text-anchor='middle'>R1=0.9</text><line x1='100' y1='50' x2='140' y2='50' stroke='#475569' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='140' y='35' width='60' height='30' fill='#e0f2fe' stroke='#475569'/><text x='170' y='54' font-size='9' text-anchor='middle'>R2=0.8</text><line x1='200' y1='50' x2='230' y2='50' stroke='#475569' stroke-width='2'/></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Series reliability is the product of component reliabilities.

**Working.**
$$R_s = R_1 R_2 = 0.9 \times 0.8 = 0.72$$

**Answer.** $R_s = \mathbf{0.72}$.

questions.subject: Mine Economics & Planning
questions.stem: The same two components (reliabilities 0.9 and 0.8) are now connected in parallel (redundant) as shown. Compute the system reliability.

figure.caption: Parallel reliability block diagram
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 140' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='10' y1='70' x2='50' y2='70' stroke='#475569' stroke-width='2'/><path d='M50 70 L50 35 L80 35' stroke='#475569' fill='none' stroke-width='2'/><path d='M50 70 L50 105 L80 105' stroke='#475569' fill='none' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='80' y='20' width='70' height='30' fill='#e0f2fe' stroke='#475569'/><text x='115' y='39' font-size='9' text-anchor='middle'>R1=0.9</text><rect x='80' y='90' width='70' height='30' fill='#e0f2fe' stroke='#475569'/><text x='115' y='109' font-size='9' text-anchor='middle'>R2=0.8</text><path d='M150 35 L180 35 L180 70' stroke='#475569' fill='none' stroke-width='2'/><path d='M150 105 L180 105 L180 70' stroke='#475569' fill='none' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='180' y1='70' x2='220' y2='70' stroke='#475569' stroke-width='2'/></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Parallel reliability: the system fails only if both fail.

**Working.**
$$R_p = 1 - (1-R_1)(1-R_2) = 1 - (0.1)(0.2) = 1 - 0.02 = 0.98$$

**Answer.** $R_p = \mathbf{0.98}$.

questions.subject: Mine Economics & Planning
questions.stem: A machine has a mean time between failures (MTBF) of 90 h and a mean time to repair (MTTR) of 10 h, as shown. Compute its steady-state availability.

figure.caption: Uptime vs downtime
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 260 90' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='20' y='35' width='180' height='20' fill='#86efac' stroke='#475569'/><rect x='200' y='35' width='30' height='20' fill='#fca5a5' stroke='#475569'/><text x='70' y='28' font-size='9'>MTBF = 90 h</text><text x='188' y='72' font-size='9'>MTTR=10 h</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Availability is the fraction of time the machine is operable.

**Working.**
$$A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \frac{90}{90 + 10} = 0.9$$

**Answer.** $A = \mathbf{0.9}$ (90%).

questions.subject: Mine Economics & Planning
questions.stem: Trucks arrive at a single loader (M/M/1) at $\lambda = 6$ per hour and are served at $\mu = 10$ per hour. Compute the server utilisation $\rho$.

figure.caption: Single-server queue
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 260 110' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><g fill='#bfdbfe' stroke='#475569'><rect x='40' y='45' width='18' height='24'/><rect x='62' y='45' width='18' height='24'/><rect x='84' y='45' width='18' height='24'/></g><circle cx='150' cy='57' r='22' fill='#fde68a' stroke='#b45309'/><text x='150' y='61' font-size='9' text-anchor='middle'>loader</text><line x1='10' y1='57' x2='38' y2='57' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#qa)'/><line x1='174' y1='57' x2='230' y2='57' stroke='#16a34a' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#qa)'/><defs><marker id='qa' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0 0 L6 3 L0 6 Z' fill='#475569'/></marker></defs><text x='12' y='44' font-size='8'>lam=6</text><text x='190' y='44' font-size='8'>mu=10</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Utilisation of an M/M/1 server is $\rho = \lambda/\mu$.

**Working.**
$$\rho = \frac{6}{10} = 0.6$$

**Answer.** $\rho = \mathbf{0.6}$.

questions.subject: Mine Economics & Planning
questions.stem: For the decision branch shown, with a 0.6 probability of a ₹100 lakh gain and a 0.4 probability of a ₹50 lakh loss, compute the Expected Monetary Value (₹ lakh).

figure.caption: Decision tree branch
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 260 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='20' y='65' width='16' height='16' fill='#1f2937'/><line x1='36' y1='73' x2='120' y2='40' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='36' y1='73' x2='120' y2='110' stroke='#475569'/><circle cx='120' cy='40' r='5' fill='#fff' stroke='#475569'/><circle cx='120' cy='110' r='5' fill='#fff' stroke='#475569'/><text x='58' y='40' font-size='8'>p=0.6</text><text x='58' y='118' font-size='8'>p=0.4</text><text x='132' y='44' font-size='8'>+100</text><text x='132' y='114' font-size='8'>-50</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** EMV is the probability-weighted sum of outcomes.

**Working.**
$$EMV = 0.6(100) + 0.4(-50) = 60 - 20 = 40$$

**Answer.** EMV $= \mathbf{40}$ ₹ lakh.

questions.subject: Mine Economics & Planning
questions.stem: Using the cash-flow timeline shown, compute the present value (₹) of ₹100 received at the end of each of the next two years at a discount rate of 10% per year.

figure.caption: Cash-flow timeline
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 120' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='20' y1='60' x2='220' y2='60' stroke='#475569'/><g fill='#1f2937'><circle cx='40' cy='60' r='3'/><circle cx='120' cy='60' r='3'/><circle cx='200' cy='60' r='3'/></g><text x='36' y='50' font-size='9'>0</text><text x='116' y='50' font-size='9'>1</text><text x='196' y='50' font-size='9'>2</text><line x1='120' y1='60' x2='120' y2='38' stroke='#16a34a' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#na)'/><line x1='200' y1='60' x2='200' y2='38' stroke='#16a34a' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#na)'/><defs><marker id='na' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='4' refY='0' orient='auto'><path d='M0 6 L4 0 L8 6 Z' fill='#16a34a'/></marker></defs><text x='150' y='95' font-size='9'>100 each year, i=10%</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Discount each cash flow to year 0 and sum.

**Working.**
$$PV = \frac{100}{1.1} + \frac{100}{1.1^2} = 90.91 + 82.64 = 173.55$$

**Answer.** PV $\approx$ ₹**173.55**.

questions.subject: Mine Economics & Planning
questions.stem: From the bar chart of present values shown (PV of benefits ₹150, PV of costs ₹100), compute the Benefit–Cost Ratio.

figure.caption: Benefit vs cost
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 200 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='30' y1='130' x2='190' y2='130' stroke='#475569'/><rect x='50' y='40' width='40' height='90' fill='#16a34a'/><rect x='120' y='70' width='40' height='60' fill='#dc2626'/><text x='48' y='32' font-size='8'>PV B=150</text><text x='116' y='62' font-size='8'>PV C=100</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** BCR is the ratio of present worth of benefits to present worth of costs.

**Working.**
$$BCR = \frac{150}{100} = 1.5$$

**Answer.** BCR $= \mathbf{1.5}$ (project is economically attractive, BCR > 1).

questions.subject: Mine Economics & Planning
questions.stem: From the inventory profile shown, with a demand rate of 50 units/day and a lead time of 4 days, compute the re-order point (units).

figure.caption: Re-order point
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 260 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='30' y1='120' x2='240' y2='120' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='30' y1='120' x2='30' y2='20' stroke='#475569'/><polyline points='30,30 110,90 110,30 190,90' fill='none' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='30' y1='90' x2='240' y2='90' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-dasharray='3 2'/><text x='150' y='86' font-size='8' fill='#dc2626'>ROP</text><text x='52' y='140' font-size='8'>demand 50/day, lead time 4 days</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Re-order point (no safety stock) equals demand during lead time.

**Working.**
$$ROP = d \times L = 50 \times 4 = 200$$

**Answer.** ROP $= \mathbf{200}$ units.

questions.subject: Mine Economics & Planning
questions.stem: In the decision framework shown, which cost is irrelevant to a forward-looking replace-or-keep decision?

figure.caption: Past vs future cost
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 110' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='20' y='40' width='90' height='40' fill='#cbd5e1' stroke='#475569'/><text x='65' y='64' font-size='9' text-anchor='middle'>past cost</text><rect x='130' y='40' width='90' height='40' fill='#bbf7d0' stroke='#16a34a'/><text x='175' y='64' font-size='9' text-anchor='middle'>future cost</text><line x1='110' y1='60' x2='128' y2='60' stroke='#475569' marker-end='url(#sc)'/><defs><marker id='sc' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0 0 L6 3 L0 6 Z' fill='#475569'/></marker></defs></svg>

options: The future operating cost of keeping the asset
The purchase price of the replacement
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Only future, decision-dependent cash flows are relevant.

**Reasoning.** A sunk cost has already been incurred and cannot be changed by any future decision, so it is irrelevant to a replace-or-keep analysis. Future operating, purchase and maintenance costs all differ between alternatives and are relevant.

**Answer.** The sunk (past) cost.

questions.stem: Actual output is $8000$ t and the break-even output is $5000$ t. Compute the margin of safety (t).

figure.caption: Margin of safety
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 120' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='20' y1='60' x2='220' y2='60' stroke='#475569'/><circle cx='110' cy='60' r='4' fill='#dc2626'/><circle cx='180' cy='60' r='4' fill='#16a34a'/><text x='90' y='50' font-size='8'>BEP 5000</text><text x='160' y='50' font-size='8'>actual 8000</text><line x1='110' y1='75' x2='180' y2='75' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='2'/><text x='118' y='95' font-size='8'>margin of safety</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Margin of safety $=$ actual output $-$ break-even output.

**Working.**
$$\text{MoS}=8000-5000=3000\ \text{t}$$

**Answer.** $3000$ t.

questions.stem: The chart shows undiscounted and discounted cumulative cash-flow curves. Compared with the simple payback, the discounted payback period is always:

figure.caption: Simple vs discounted payback
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='30' y1='120' x2='220' y2='120' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='30' y1='120' x2='30' y2='20' stroke='#475569'/><polyline points='30,120 70,98 110,76 150,54 190,34' fill='none' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='2'/><polyline points='30,120 70,106 110,92 150,80 190,68' fill='none' stroke='#16a34a' stroke-width='2'/><text x='40' y='40' font-size='8'>undiscounted</text><text x='115' y='105' font-size='8'>discounted</text></svg>

options: longer, because discounting reduces the value of later inflows
shorter, because discounting increases later inflows
exactly equal to the simple payback
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Discounting lowers the present value of future inflows.

**Reasoning.** Since each year's inflow contributes less in present-value terms, the cumulative discounted curve rises more slowly and reaches the investment line later than the undiscounted curve.

**Answer.** Longer than the simple payback period.

questions.subject: Mine Economics & Planning
questions.stem: On the NPV-versus-discount-rate curve shown, the internal rate of return (IRR) is the rate at which:

figure.caption: NPV profile and IRR
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='30' y1='80' x2='220' y2='80' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='30' y1='130' x2='30' y2='20' stroke='#475569'/><path d='M40 30 Q120 90 200 120' fill='none' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='150' cy='80' r='3' fill='#dc2626'/><text x='150' y='72' font-size='8'>IRR</text><text x='34' y='28' font-size='8'>NPV</text><text x='188' y='95' font-size='8'>rate</text></svg>

options: the NPV equals the initial investment
questions.solution:
**Concept.** IRR is the discount rate giving zero net present value.

**Reasoning.** On the NPV profile, increasing the discount rate lowers NPV; the rate at which the curve intersects the horizontal (zero) axis is the IRR.

**Answer.** The NPV curve crosses zero.

questions.subject: Mine Economics & Planning
questions.stem: Ore value is $\textsf{Rs}\,50$/t and ore mining + processing cost is $\textsf{Rs}\,30$/t, while waste removal costs $\textsf{Rs}\,4$/t. Compute the break-even (maximum allowable) stripping ratio (t waste per t ore).

figure.caption: Break-even stripping ratio
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><polygon points='20,40 220,40 180,95 60,95' fill='#cbd5e1' stroke='#475569'/><rect x='80' y='95' width='80' height='25' fill='#92400e' stroke='#475569'/><text x='80' y='30' font-size='8'>value 50, ore cost 30, waste 4/t</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** BESR $=\dfrac{\text{ore value}-\text{ore cost}}{\text{waste cost per t}}$.

**Working.**
$$\text{BESR}=\frac{50-30}{4}=5$$

**Answer.** $5:1$.

questions.subject: Mine Economics & Planning
questions.stem: The combined mining + processing cost is $\textsf{Rs}\,40$/t, the metal price gives $\textsf{Rs}\,100$ per unit grade per tonne, and mill recovery is $0.80$. Compute the break-even cut-off grade (units).

figure.caption: Cut-off grade from cost line
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='30' y1='120' x2='200' y2='120' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='30' y1='120' x2='30' y2='20' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='30' y1='100' x2='200' y2='40' stroke='#16a34a' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='30' y1='80' x2='200' y2='80' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-dasharray='3 2'/><text x='34' y='75' font-size='8'>cost</text><text x='110' y='140' font-size='8'>grade -> recoverable value</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** At cut-off, recoverable value equals cost: $g\times\text{price}\times\text{recovery}=\text{cost}$.

**Working.**
$$g=\frac{40}{100\times0.80}=\frac{40}{80}=0.5$$

**Answer.** $0.5$ units.

questions.subject: Mine Economics & Planning
questions.stem: Taylor's rule, illustrated by the trend shown, relates optimum mine life to ore tonnage. According to the rule, as the deposit tonnage increases, the optimum mine life:

figure.caption: Taylor's rule trend
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 140' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='30' y1='110' x2='200' y2='110' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='30' y1='110' x2='30' y2='20' stroke='#475569'/><path d='M35 100 Q110 60 195 35' fill='none' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='2'/><text x='34' y='30' font-size='8'>life</text><text x='150' y='125' font-size='8'>tonnage</text></svg>

options: increases (roughly with the fourth root of tonnage)
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Taylor's rule: optimum life $\approx 0.2\,(\text{tonnage})^{0.25}$.

**Reasoning.** Life grows with the fourth root of tonnage, so larger deposits warrant longer lives but with diminishing increments, giving the concave rising curve shown.

**Answer.** It increases, roughly with the fourth root of tonnage.

questions.subject: Mine Economics & Planning
questions.stem: In the activity network shown, the upper path durations are $6$ and $9$ days and the lower path durations are $4$ and $5$ days between the same start and end nodes. Compute the project (critical-path) duration in days.

figure.caption: Activity network
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 260 140' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><g fill='#bfdbfe' stroke='#475569'><circle cx='30' cy='70' r='14'/><circle cx='110' cy='40' r='14'/><circle cx='110' cy='100' r='14'/><circle cx='200' cy='70' r='14'/></g><g stroke='#475569' marker-end='url(#na)'><line x1='44' y1='65' x2='96' y2='45'/><line x1='44' y1='75' x2='96' y2='95'/><line x1='124' y1='45' x2='186' y2='65'/><line x1='124' y1='95' x2='186' y2='75'/></g><defs><marker id='na' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='7' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0 0 L6 3 L0 6 Z' fill='#475569'/></marker></defs><text x='62' y='40' font-size='8'>6</text><text x='62' y='118' font-size='8'>4</text><text x='160' y='42' font-size='8'>9</text><text x='160' y='112' font-size='8'>5</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** The critical path is the longest path through the network.

**Working.**
$$\text{upper}=6+9=15,\quad \text{lower}=4+5=9\Rightarrow \text{duration}=15$$

**Answer.** $15$ days.

questions.subject: Mine Economics & Planning
questions.stem: For the same network (critical path $15$ days, lower path $4+5=9$ days), compute the total float available on the lower path (days).

figure.caption: Total float on a non-critical path
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 260 140' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><g fill='#bfdbfe' stroke='#475569'><circle cx='30' cy='70' r='14'/><circle cx='110' cy='40' r='14'/><circle cx='110' cy='100' r='14'/><circle cx='200' cy='70' r='14'/></g><g stroke='#dc2626' marker-end='url(#na)'><line x1='44' y1='75' x2='96' y2='95'/><line x1='124' y1='95' x2='186' y2='75'/></g><g stroke='#475569' marker-end='url(#na)'><line x1='44' y1='65' x2='96' y2='45'/><line x1='124' y1='45' x2='186' y2='65'/></g><defs><marker id='na' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='7' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0 0 L6 3 L0 6 Z' fill='#475569'/></marker></defs><text x='150' y='112' font-size='8'>lower path = 9</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Total float $=$ critical-path duration $-$ path duration for parallel chains sharing the same end nodes.

**Working.**
$$\text{float}=15-9=6\ \text{days}$$

**Answer.** $6$ days.

questions.subject: Mine Economics & Planning
questions.stem: The activities on a critical path have variances summing to $4\ \mathrm{days^2}$. Compute the standard deviation of the project completion time (days).

figure.caption: Project duration distribution
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 120' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='20' y1='100' x2='200' y2='100' stroke='#475569'/><path d='M30 100 Q110 20 190 100' fill='none' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='2'/><text x='70' y='118' font-size='8'>sum of variances = 4</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Project standard deviation $=\sqrt{\sum\sigma_i^2}$ along the critical path.

**Working.**
$$\sigma=\sqrt{4}=2\ \text{days}$$

**Answer.** $2$ days.

questions.subject: Mine Economics & Planning
questions.stem: For the linear-programming feasible region shown (shaded polygon), the maximum of a linear objective function occurs:

figure.caption: LP feasible region
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='30' y1='130' x2='210' y2='130' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='30' y1='130' x2='30' y2='20' stroke='#475569'/><polygon points='30,130 30,60 90,40 140,70 160,130' fill='#bfdbfe' stroke='#2563eb'/><circle cx='90' cy='40' r='3' fill='#dc2626'/><text x='72' y='34' font-size='8'>vertex</text><text x='110' y='150' font-size='8'>feasible region</text></svg>

options: at one of the corner points (vertices) of the feasible region
always at the centroid of the region
always in the interior, never on the boundary
questions.solution:
**Concept.** The fundamental theorem of linear programming.

**Reasoning.** A linear objective over a convex polygonal feasible region attains its optimum at a vertex (corner) of the region; interior points can never beat the best corner.

**Answer.** At a corner point (vertex) of the feasible region.

questions.subject: Mine Economics & Planning
questions.stem: At the optimum vertex of the LP shown, two constraint lines intersect. These constraints are described as:
questions.solution:
**Concept.** A constraint satisfied as an equality at the optimum is binding.

**Reasoning.** The two lines passing through the optimum vertex hold with equality there, so they are the binding (active) constraints that determine the solution.

**Answer.** Binding (active) constraints.

questions.subject: Mine Economics & Planning
questions.stem: Ore yields a recoverable value of Rs $50$ per tonne against an ore production cost of Rs $20$ per tonne, while waste removal costs Rs $6$ per tonne (figure). Compute the break-even stripping ratio (tonnes waste per tonne ore).

figure.caption: Waste over ore
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 140' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><polygon points='20,40 220,40 180,90 60,90' fill='#cbd5e1' stroke='#475569'/><rect x='80' y='90' width='80' height='25' fill='#92400e' stroke='#475569'/><text x='84' y='66' font-size='8'>waste, cost 6/unit</text><text x='92' y='107' font-size='8' fill='#fff'>ore value 50</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Break-even SR $=\dfrac{\text{value}-\text{ore cost}}{\text{waste cost}}$.

**Working.**
$$SR=\frac{50-20}{6}=\frac{30}{6}=5$$

**Answer.** $5$ t waste per t ore.

questions.subject: Mine Economics & Planning
questions.stem: Total operating cost for a period is Rs $600000$ for an output of $30000\ \text{t}$ (figure). Compute the unit cost (Rs/t).

figure.caption: Unit cost
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 140' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='40' y='40' width='120' height='60' fill='#bfdbfe' stroke='#475569'/><text x='55' y='75' font-size='9'>total cost 600000</text><text x='44' y='125' font-size='8'>output 30000 t -> unit cost?</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Unit cost $=\dfrac{\text{total cost}}{\text{output}}$.

**Working.**
$$c=\frac{600000}{30000}=20\ \text{Rs/t}$$

**Answer.** Rs $20$ per tonne.

questions.stem: The critical path of a project runs through three activities in series with durations $A=4$, $B=6$ and $C=5$ days (figure). Compute the project duration (days).

figure.caption: Series activity network
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 140' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><g fill='#bfdbfe' stroke='#475569'><circle cx='30' cy='70' r='14'/><circle cx='110' cy='70' r='14'/><circle cx='190' cy='70' r='14'/></g><line x1='44' y1='70' x2='96' y2='70' stroke='#475569' marker-end='url(#a)'/><line x1='124' y1='70' x2='176' y2='70' stroke='#475569' marker-end='url(#a)'/><defs><marker id='a' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0 0 L6 3 L0 6 Z' fill='#475569'/></marker></defs><text x='56' y='60' font-size='8'>A=4</text><text x='136' y='60' font-size='8'>B=6</text><text x='92' y='112' font-size='8'>then C=5 from last node</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Project duration equals the sum of durations along the critical (longest) path.

**Working.**
$$T=4+6+5=15\ \text{days}$$

**Answer.** $15$ days.

questions.subject: Mine Economics & Planning
questions.stem: The discounted cash-flow diagram shown gives a positive net present value at the chosen discount rate (figure). The correct decision is to:

figure.caption: NPV decision
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 140' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='30' y1='75' x2='200' y2='75' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='30' y1='30' x2='30' y2='120' stroke='#475569'/><rect x='60' y='45' width='25' height='30' fill='#16a34a'/><rect x='110' y='75' width='25' height='25' fill='#dc2626'/><text x='40' y='28' font-size='8'>NPV at chosen rate</text><text x='52' y='135' font-size='8'>accept if NPV > 0</text></svg>

options: accept the project, since a positive NPV means it adds value at the required rate of return
reject the project, since any NPV indicates loss
remain indifferent, since NPV is irrelevant to the decision
accept only if the payback exceeds the mine life
questions.solution:
**Concept.** NPV decision rule.

**Reasoning.** A positive NPV means the discounted inflows exceed the discounted outflows at the required rate, so the project adds value and should be accepted.

**Answer.** Accept the project, since a positive NPV means it adds value at the required rate of return.
